title: NOC 74205 – Public works maintenance equipment operators and related workers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 74203 professionals are the mechanical heart of Canada’s municipal upkeep. As a TEER 4 occupation, these operators use specialized heavy and light equipment to maintain roads, clean public spaces, and manage waste. In 2026, as Canada moves toward Zero-Emission Municipal Fleets and "Smart City" maintenance protocols, these operators are classified as Essential Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a primary target for the Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot and the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level4Major Group74 – Mail and message distribution, transport equipment operators and maintenanceSub-Major Group742 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersMinor Group7420 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersNOC Code74205

duties: Equipment Operation: Operate specialized vehicles and equipment such as street sweepers, snowplows, and garbage trucks to maintain public infrastructure.
Waste Management: Collect and transport refuse and recyclable materials to disposal or recycling facilities.
Seasonal Maintenance: Clear snow and ice from roads, sidewalks, and parking lots using plows, blowers, and salt/sand spreaders.
Basic Repairs: Perform minor maintenance and repairs on equipment, such as changing brushes on sweepers or lubricating moving parts.
Safety & Inspection: Conduct pre-trip and post-trip inspections of vehicles to ensure mechanical safety and compliance with provincial regulations.
Routing & Tech: Use GPS-based routing and material-tracking software to optimize collection paths and record salt/sand usage.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Licensing (Mandatory): * A valid Class 3 Driver’s License (with Air Brake "Z" endorsement) is mandatory for most heavy equipment.A clean driving record (Driver’s Abstract) is essential.
A clean driving record (Driver’s Abstract) is essential.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are provided by the municipality or private contractor.
Experience: Previous experience as a public works labourer (NOC 75212) is often preferred for operator roles.
Physical stamina for long shifts and the ability to work in extreme weather conditions (especially during winter emergencies) are mandatory.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, unionized municipal operators in major cities often earn annual salaries between $62,000 and $88,000 inclusive of overtime premiums and shift differentials.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the shift toward "Smart Waste Management"—using bin sensors and AI-optimized routing—will require operators to be more tech-literate. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), Retirements will create over 18,000 job openings across Canada through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The demand for public works equipment operators in 2026 is Very Good, particularly in high-growth urban corridors and northern communities. In Ontario, British Columbia, and Alberta, municipal governments are facing a "Replacement Crisis" as senior operators retire. According to 2026 Job Bank data, there is a persistent labor shortage for operators specialized in Winter Maintenance Technology (precision salt spreaders) and Electric Waste Management Vehicles.
Modernized Fleet Operations: Gain experience operating the latest hybrid and fully electric street sweepers, snowplows, and refuse trucks.
Public Sector Stability: Many roles are within municipal or provincial governments, offering superior job security, union protection, and comprehensive pension plans.
Predictable Work-Life Balance: Unlike private construction, municipal roles often offer stable, set shifts with predictable overtime during seasonal peaks.

Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 4 role is a direct beneficiary of the 2026 federal and provincial pilots designed to retain essential service workers who have at least one year of Canadian experience.
pathways: New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, targeting those in TEER 4 and 5 roles with one year of experience.
Saskatchewan SINP (Hard-to-Fill Skills): A high-priority route for equipment operators with a valid job offer in the province.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Ideal for operators currently employed in British Columbia for at least 9 months.
OINP In-Demand Skills Stream: Frequently targets operators in the transportation and municipal sectors for regions outside the GTA.
